participants.extracted: Adults aged 18 years or older who smoked at least five cigarettes per day and were highly motivated to quit smoking were recruited from the Madison–Dane County area using advertisements, with efforts to reach low socioeconomic status neighborhoods. Participants had to be willing to attend ten meetings over about two months. Exclusion criteria at phone screening included use of other tobacco products and heavy alcohol use (four or more drinks on four or more nights per week). At the orientation visit, people with suicidal ideation or a PHQ-9 score of 9 or higher were excluded and referred for care. No screening excluded homelessness, psychiatric disorders, or comorbid drug use. Of 943 callers, 730 were screened by phone and 196 were randomized (105 to Mindfulness Training for Smokers and 91 to control Quit Line plus nicotine replacement therapy). The randomized sample had a mean age of 41.65 years, was 50% male and 50% female, and was socioeconomically disadvantaged relative to typical smoking-study samples, with less than half having education beyond high school and a higher proportion of African American participants. Treatment initiators numbered 118 (59 per group).

interventions.extracted: Intervention: Mindfulness Training for Smokers (MTS), a mindfulness-based smoking cessation program for low-SES adult smokers. MTS was delivered over 7 weeks and included a 7-hour introductory class, four weekday 90-minute classes with an hour-long meditation/support group after each, and a 7-hour Quit Day Retreat; after the retreat participants also attended weekly meditation group support. The program used an instructional DVD/manual, simple language, and concrete exercises, and taught mindfulness practices such as meditation, mindful walking, mindful eating, mindful talking/listening, and applying mindfulness to smoking triggers, urges, withdrawal symptoms, addictive thoughts, stress, and negative emotions. Participants were asked to practice 30 minutes of guided meditation daily using a CD and received a 4-week course of 21 mg nicotine patches starting at quit day. Comparison: usual care via the Wisconsin Tobacco Quit Line plus 4 weeks of nicotine replacement therapy, with participants instructed to call a quit coach and pick up nicotine patches.

outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: biochemically confirmed 7-day point-prevalence smoking abstinence at 4 weeks and 24 weeks after the scheduled quit day, assessed with carbon monoxide (CO) breath testing and Time-Line Follow-Back (TLFB), with a CO cutoff of <7 ppm. Secondary smoking outcomes included continuous abstinence at 4 weeks and 24 weeks (using TLFB, CO, and self-report of no smoking since quit day at 24 weeks) and number of days smoked in the first two weeks post-quit. Treatment acceptability outcomes for the intervention group included class attendance, intervention completion at the Quit Day Retreat, and daily minutes of meditation recorded on a Meditation Calendar/TLFB during the intervention and for one month after quit day. Other secondary self-report outcomes measured at baseline, 4 weeks, and 24 weeks post-quit were Difficulty in Emotion Regulation Scale (DERS), Attentional Control Scale (ACS), and Five Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire (FFMQ) composite and subscales (observing, describing, acting with awareness, non-judging of inner experience, non-reactivity to inner experience). Additional baseline measures included demographics and smoking history, Fagerström Test for Nicotine Dependence (FTND), and Brief Wisconsin Inventory of Smoking Dependence Motives (WISDM).
